edges.justification: Real yields represent the opportunity cost of holding non-yielding gold; when real yields rise, gold becomes less attractive relative to TIPS, and vice versa. This is one of the most well-documented causal relationships in the gold literature.
The Fed Funds rate raises the return on cash and short-term instruments relative to gold, directly affecting gold's opportunity cost and thus demand.
The Fed Funds rate anchors the short end of the yield curve and transmits to longer-dated real yields through the expectations channel and term-premium effects.
Monetary policy directly shapes inflation expectations; a tighter Fed Funds rate signals commitment to lower inflation, compressing break-even inflation, while easing raises inflation expectations.
Break-even inflation captures market-implied inflation expectations; rising expectations increase demand for inflation-hedge assets like gold, pushing prices higher.

By construction, the 10-year nominal yield equals the real yield plus break-even inflation, so movements in break-even directly affect the decomposition of nominal yields into real and inflation components.
Realized inflation erodes the purchasing power of fiat currency, triggering inflation-hedge demand for gold as a store of real value.
Realized CPI prints update market participants' beliefs about future inflation, causing break-even inflation expectations to adjust in the same direction as realized outcomes.

The Federal Reserve sets the Fed Funds rate in direct response to realized inflation outcomes as part of its price-stability mandate; higher CPI inflation systematically causes the Fed to raise rates.
Realized inflation influences market expectations about future Fed policy and the real return environment, shifting real yields as investors reprice TIPS against nominal bonds.
Gold is priced in U.S. dollars globally; a stronger dollar mechanically makes gold more expensive for foreign buyers, reducing demand and depressing prices, while a weaker dollar boosts demand.
Higher U.S. interest rates relative to foreign rates attract capital inflows into dollar-denominated assets, strengthening the dollar through the interest-rate parity and capital-flow channels.
Higher U.S. real yields attract global capital seeking real returns, increasing demand for dollar-denominated assets and strengthening the dollar.
Strong equity returns drive capital toward risk assets and away from safe-haven gold (risk-on/risk-off rotation), while equity selloffs trigger safe-haven demand for gold.
Rising VIX signals elevated financial-market stress and risk aversion, triggering flight-to-safety demand for gold as investors seek assets uncorrelated with equity drawdowns.
VIX is derived from S&P 500 option prices and spikes during equity market selloffs; contemporaneously, high VIX periods correspond to negative equity returns and vice versa.
Widening credit spreads signal rising corporate credit stress and systemic financial risk, driving safe-haven demand for gold as investors seek assets with no counterparty risk.

Credit stress and equity-market stress are simultaneously driven by broader financial-system risk; widening IG spreads and rising VIX co-move during periods of financial distress and each amplifies the other.

Widening credit spreads signal deteriorating corporate financial conditions, which depress equity valuations; conversely, equity selloffs can feed back into credit spread widening through balance-sheet and confidence channels.
Rising debt-to-GDP signals fiscal unsustainability and heightens long-run debasement risk for fiat currency, driving demand for gold as a hedge against sovereign credit deterioration.
High government debt levels raise the probability of inflation as a debt-management tool, pushing long-term inflation expectations upward as reflected in break-even rates.
Elevated fiscal deficits and rising debt-to-GDP can push up the term premium on long-dated Treasuries, influencing real yields through increased supply of government debt and sovereign risk concerns.

Oil prices influence gold through the production-cost channel (energy is a major mining input) and through the inflation channel (oil price shocks feed into CPI, triggering inflation-hedge demand for gold).
Energy prices are a direct component of CPI and feed through to broader consumer prices via transportation and production costs; oil price increases mechanically raise realized inflation.
Oil price changes rapidly shift market participants' expectations for near- and medium-term inflation, causing break-even inflation rates to adjust in line with energy-price movements.
Global economic expansion (CLI above trend) rotates investor capital toward growth-sensitive assets and away from safe-haven gold, while contraction signals raise safe-haven gold demand.
The OECD CLI is designed to lead economic turning points; improving global economic prospects support corporate earnings expectations, positively influencing equity market returns.
Global economic activity is the primary driver of oil demand; a rising CLI indicating above-trend global growth increases energy consumption and supports oil prices.

The CLI is designed to lead real-economy turning points; deteriorating global conditions captured by a falling CLI precede rising domestic unemployment, while expansion corresponds to tightening labor markets.

Mine production is the primary source of new gold supply; holding demand constant, higher production increases available supply and exerts downward pressure on price, while lower production constrains supply and supports higher prices.
Central bank net buying directly adds to physical gold demand from a large, strategic buyer base; net purchases support higher prices while net selling pressures prices downward.

ETF net inflows physically remove gold from available market supply as funds must hold bullion to back new shares, directly adding to demand and supporting prices; outflows release supply and depress prices.
Jewelry consumption is the largest single category of physical gold demand; higher jewelry offtake tightens the physical gold market and supports prices, while weak demand eases price pressure.

Gold price levels inversely affect jewelry demand through a standard price-quantity mechanism: higher gold prices reduce affordability for price-sensitive consumers in India, China, and the Middle East, reducing offtake.
Rising gold prices can attract momentum-driven institutional inflows into gold ETFs, while falling prices can trigger redemptions; price momentum affects investor sentiment toward ETF products.
Geopolitical risk events trigger flight-to-safety demand for gold as investors seek tangible stores of value uncorrelated with politically exposed assets or specific geographies.
Major geopolitical risk events (wars, terrorist attacks, military escalations) increase equity-market uncertainty and spike VIX as investors reprice the risk premium on equities.

Geopolitical events in oil-producing regions directly disrupt supply or raise supply-disruption risk premiums, causing oil price spikes; conversely, oil price shocks can generate geopolitical tensions.

Elevated geopolitical risk motivates central banks — particularly emerging-market central banks — to accumulate gold reserves as a hedge against sanctions risk and geopolitical exposure of FX reserves.
Geopolitical stress often triggers a flight to dollar-denominated safe assets, strengthening the DXY as global investors seek dollar liquidity during crisis episodes.
Economic policy uncertainty raises the risk premium on policy-exposed assets and drives demand for gold as an asset with no counterparty risk or direct exposure to any single policy regime.
Policy uncertainty raises the risk premium investors require on equities and other risky assets, elevating implied volatility as reflected in VIX during high-uncertainty policy episodes.
Elevated economic policy uncertainty depresses equity valuations by raising discount rates and reducing confidence in corporate earnings forecasts, generating negative equity returns.
Policy uncertainty raises the risk premium on corporate debt by increasing uncertainty around regulatory, fiscal, and monetary environments, widening investment-grade credit spreads.
Policy uncertainty drives institutional investors toward gold ETFs as a hedge against policy regime risk, increasing ETF inflows during high-EPU episodes.
Consumer pessimism about multi-year economic conditions drives retail demand for defensive stores of value including gold bars, coins, and ETF holdings, supporting gold prices.
High consumer pessimism about future economic conditions reduces discretionary spending including jewelry purchases, directly reducing gold jewelry demand.
Consumer pessimism reflects and reinforces negative economic expectations, weighing on equity market sentiment and contributing to negative equity returns.

Silver and gold share inflation-hedge, safe-haven, and monetary-debasement demand drivers, so shocks that drive one precious metal tend to spill over to the other through shared investor demand channels.
Rising unemployment signals recession risk and triggers monetary easing expectations and safe-haven demand for gold simultaneously through the monetary-policy and risk-aversion channels.

The unemployment rate is a primary input into Fed monetary policy under its dual mandate; rising unemployment causes the Fed to cut rates, while falling unemployment permits or necessitates tightening.

Unemployment and inflation are linked through the Phillips curve mechanism; lower unemployment tightens labor markets and generates wage-push inflation, while higher unemployment reduces inflationary pressure.

Rising unemployment signals deteriorating economic conditions and increases the probability of corporate default, mechanically widening credit spreads as the macroeconomic risk environment deteriorates.
Labor market conditions directly shape consumer economic expectations; rising unemployment causes households to view future economic prospects as worse, increasing the share expecting bad times.
Higher unemployment reduces household income and consumer confidence, suppressing discretionary spending on jewelry and reducing physical gold demand through this channel.
Deteriorating global economic conditions (falling CLI) increase corporate default risk and tighten financial conditions, causing credit spreads to widen; improving conditions compress spreads.
Global economic expansion supports emerging-market consumer incomes — particularly in India and China — increasing discretionary spending on gold jewelry; contraction reduces this demand.

Rising sovereign debt levels can raise concerns about fiscal dominance and indirectly elevate the risk premium across the credit spectrum, potentially widening corporate credit spreads through contagion and confidence channels.
Major geopolitical events create policy uncertainty about government responses (sanctions, defense spending, regulatory changes), causing EPU to rise contemporaneously with geopolitical risk spikes.
Geopolitical stress raises systemic financial risk and uncertainty about corporate operating environments, widening credit spreads as investors demand higher compensation for holding corporate debt.
Energy is a major variable cost in gold mining operations; sustained increases in oil prices raise production costs and can constrain or reduce mine output at the margin.
Silver and gold are frequently co-produced from the same polymetallic deposits; changes in mining activity driven by one metal's economics affect the joint supply of both metals.
High economic policy uncertainty, particularly around U.S. fiscal and monetary policy, motivates central banks to diversify reserves away from dollar-denominated assets toward gold.
Consumer pessimism about multi-year economic conditions drives retail and household investors toward defensive assets including gold ETFs, increasing net inflows during high-pessimism periods.
Elevated equity-market volatility and financial stress drive institutional and retail investors into gold ETFs as a safe-haven hedge, generating net inflows during high-VIX periods.

Equity market stress and credit market stress are simultaneously manifestations of broader financial-system risk aversion; VIX spikes and credit spread widening are strongly co-determined during risk-off episodes.
When real yields rise, the opportunity cost of holding non-yielding gold increases, prompting institutional investors to reduce gold ETF positions; falling real yields attract ETF inflows.
Oil is priced in dollars globally; a stronger dollar makes oil more expensive for foreign buyers, reducing international demand and putting downward pressure on dollar-denominated oil prices.

A stronger dollar reduces the purchasing power of non-dollar consumers for dollar-priced gold, suppressing jewelry demand in key markets like India and China where incomes are denominated in local currencies.

A rapidly rising debt-to-GDP ratio can erode foreign investor confidence in the long-run value of the dollar, generating selling pressure on the DXY as reserve managers diversify away from dollar assets.
